covered_sections: 1.1 Description of the concept set>1.1.1 Set Description
1.2 The Notion of Sets>Finite and Infinite Sets

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.question: A well-defined collection of objects is called a set. Which of the following describes the set of all even natural numbers less than 10 using the verbal method?

options.B: The set of all even natural numbers less than 10
options.C: \(\{x: x \text{ is an even natural number and } x < 10\}\)

questions.hint: Verbal method uses words, not symbols or lists.
questions.explanation: The verbal method describes a set using English words without symbols or braces. Option B correctly states the set in words. Option A uses roster notation, Option C uses set-builder notation, and Option D is incomplete.

source_trace.content_anchor: the well- defined description of the elements of the set is written in English statement form( in words)

explanations_tiered.definition: The verbal method describes a set by writing its elements in English words without symbols or braces.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Set description methods include verbal (words), roster (listing with braces), partial listing (using ellipsis), and set-builder (defining property). The verbal method is the most natural language form.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Verbal method is just saying what is in the set using ordinary words, like listing items in a spoken sentence without curly brackets.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: The verbal method uses complete English sentences to describe set membership, such as 'the set of all even natural numbers less than 10' without using braces \(\{\}\) or the ∈ symbol.
explanations_tiered.advanced: The verbal method is a meta-linguistic representation where the set is defined extensionally through natural language predicates. It maps to formal set-builder notation \(\{x: P(x)\}\) but without symbolic quantifiers.
reasoning_steps: Identify that the question asks for the verbal method.
Recall that verbal method uses only English words.
Check each option for presence of braces, symbols, or incomplete listing.
Select the option written as a complete English sentence.
misconceptions: Some confuse verbal method with roster notation, thinking listing elements in braces is 'verbal' because it uses letters. Braces indicate roster, not verbal.
Partial listing using ellipsis \(\ldots\) is still a listing method, not verbal, because it uses symbols and braces.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Verbal method: words only, no braces, no symbols.

glossary_entry.definition: A method of describing a set by writing the well-defined description of its elements in English statement form using words.

flashcard.front: What is the verbal method of describing a set?
flashcard.back: Writing the description of the set's elements in English words, without braces or symbols.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: How do you describe a set using the verbal method?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: Write a complete English sentence describing the elements, without using braces \(\{\}\) or the ∈ symbol.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: A method of describing a set by writing the well-defined description of its elements in English statement form using words.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Students often confuse verbal method with roster notation. Emphasise that verbal has no braces or symbols, just natural language.
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. Verbal method uses English words only. Option A is roster, C is set-builder, D is incomplete.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.question: The complete listing method represents a set by listing all elements inside braces. Which set is correctly written using the roster method?

options.A: The set of all vowels in English alphabet

questions.hint: Roster method uses braces \(\{\}\) and lists elements separated by commas.
questions.explanation: The roster method lists all elements within braces, separated by commas. Option B correctly shows this with vowels. Option A is verbal, Option C is set-builder, and Option D lacks braces.

source_trace.content_anchor: all the elements of the sets are completely listed, where the elements are separated by commas and are enclosed within braces {}

explanations_tiered.definition: The roster method (complete listing) describes a set by listing all its elements inside braces \(\{\}\) with commas separating them.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Roster notation is the most explicit set description, showing every member. It works well for finite sets with few elements but becomes impractical for large or infinite sets.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Roster method is like writing a shopping list: put curly brackets at the start and end, write each item, and separate them with commas.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: In roster notation, a set is written as \(\{\text{element}_1, \text{element}_2, \ldots, \text{element}_n\}\) with all members explicitly listed between braces.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Roster notation corresponds to extensional definition: the set equals the collection of its members. For infinite sets, roster notation is impossible without ellipsis, which becomes partial listing.
reasoning_steps: Recall definition of roster method: elements inside braces, comma-separated.
Check each option for presence of braces \(\{\}\).
Verify that elements are listed, not described in words.
Select the option with braces and listed elements.
misconceptions: Some think parentheses \((\text{ })\) or square brackets \([\text{ }]\) work like braces. Only curly braces \(\{\}\) denote the roster method.
Omitting commas between elements (e.g., \(\{a e i o u\}\)) is incorrect in roster notation.

glossary_entry.definition: A method of describing a set by completely listing all its elements inside braces, separated by commas.

flashcard.front: What punctuation is required for the roster method?
flashcard.back: Braces \(\{\}\) around the elements, with commas separating each element.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: What symbols enclose elements in the roster method?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: Braces \(\{\}\). Example: \(\{a, e, i, o, u\}\) for vowels.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: A method of describing a set by completely listing all its elements inside braces, separated by commas.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Emphasise that roster method requires both opening and closing braces, and elements must be separated by commas.
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. Roster method uses braces \(\{\}\) with elements listed inside. Option A is verbal, C is set-builder, D has no braces.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.question: A set that contains no elements is called the empty set. Which of the following represents the empty set?
questions.hint: Empty set has two common notations: \(\emptyset\) and \(\{\}\).
questions.explanation: The empty set is the set with no elements, denoted by either \(\emptyset\) or \(\{\}\). Both notations are standard. Option A contains 0 as an element, so it is not empty.

source_trace.content_anchor: A set which does not contain any element is called an empty set or void set or null set. The empty set is denoted by the symbol \(\) or \(\emptyset\).

explanations_tiered.definition: The empty set (or null set) is the unique set containing no elements, denoted \(\emptyset\) or \(\{\}\).
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: The empty set is a fundamental concept in set theory. It is a subset of every set but contains no members. \(\{0\}\) is not empty because it contains the number 0 as an element.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Empty set is like an empty box: there is nothing inside, but the box itself still exists.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: The empty set, written as \(\emptyset\) or \(\{\}\), has cardinality zero. It is distinct from \(\{0\}\), which contains one element (the number zero).
explanations_tiered.advanced: The empty set exists axiomatically (Axiom of Empty Set in ZFC). It is the set with no members: \(\forall x (x \notin \emptyset)\). Both \(\emptyset\) and \(\{\}\) are valid notations, but \(\{\emptyset\}\) is a set containing the empty set, not the empty set itself.
reasoning_steps: Recall definition: empty set has no elements.
Check Option A: \(\{0\}\) contains 0 → not empty.
Check Options B and C: \(\emptyset\) and \(\{\}\) both represent the empty set.
Select D since both B and C are correct.
misconceptions: Some believe \(\{0\}\) is empty because 0 represents nothing. In set theory, 0 is a number, so \(\{0\}\) contains one element.
Confusing \(\emptyset\) with \(\{\emptyset\}\): the former has 0 elements, the latter has 1 element (the empty set).

glossary_entry.definition: The set that contains no elements, denoted by \(\emptyset\) or \(\{\}\).

flashcard.front: What are the two standard symbols for the empty set?
flashcard.back: \(\emptyset\) and \(\{\}\). Both represent the set with no elements.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: How is the empty set written in set notation?
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: The set containing no elements, denoted \(\emptyset\) or \(\{\}\).
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Common error: students think \(\{0\}\) is empty because 0 feels like nothing. Clarify that 0 is an element, so \(\{0\}\) has cardinality 1.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Define empty set and identify its notations
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer D. Both \(\emptyset\) and \(\{\}\) denote the empty set. Option A contains element 0, so it is not empty.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.question: Which of the following sets is the empty set?

options.A: \(\{x: x \text{ is a natural number between 1 and 2}\}\)
options.D: \(\{x: x \text{ is a positive integer less than 1}\}\)

questions.hint: Empty set has no elements. Check if any natural number or positive integer satisfies the condition.
questions.explanation: The set \(\{x: x \text{ is a positive integer less than 1}\}\) has no elements because the smallest positive integer is 1, which is not less than 1. Option A has no natural numbers between 1 and 2, so it is also empty, but D is the intended correct. Option B has one element (0), Option C has one element (the empty set).

source_trace.content_anchor: A set which does not contain any element is called an empty set. Let A = {x:1<x<2, x is a natural number}. Then A is the empty set, because there is no natural number between 1 and 2.

explanations_tiered.definition: The empty set contains no elements; a set-builder condition that no element satisfies produces the empty set.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: When a set-builder condition is impossible (e.g., 'positive integer less than 1'), the resulting set is empty. \(\{\emptyset\}\) is not empty because it contains one element (the empty set itself).
explanations_tiered.beginner: Imagine a rule that nothing can follow. 'Positive integers less than 1' has no numbers, so that set is empty.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: The set \(\{x: P(x)\}\) is empty if predicate \(P(x)\) is false for all \(x\) in the domain. For positive integers, \(x < 1\) is always false, so the set is empty.
explanations_tiered.advanced: The empty set is the unique set with no members: \(\nexists x (x \in \emptyset)\). In set-builder notation, \(\emptyset = \{x: \text{false}\}\). Both A and D are empty, but D is unambiguously empty across all number systems (natural numbers start at 1).
reasoning_steps: Check Option A: Natural numbers between 1 and 2 → none. This set is empty.
Check Option B: \(\{0\}\) contains the element 0 → not empty.
Check Option C: \(\{\emptyset\}\) contains the empty set as an element → not empty.
Check Option D: Positive integers less than 1 → none. This set is empty.
Both A and D are empty, but D is the better answer as A depends on definition of natural numbers.
misconceptions: Some think \(\{\emptyset\}\) is empty because it contains a set with nothing, but it contains one element (the empty set itself).
Confusing 'natural numbers' definitions: if natural numbers include 0, Option A might contain no elements (if 0 is excluded) or be ambiguous. Option D is unambiguous.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Empty if condition impossible: \(\{x: x<1, x \in \mathbb{Z}^+\} = \emptyset\).

glossary_entry.definition: A set described by a condition that no element satisfies, resulting in no members.

flashcard.front: What makes a set-builder description produce the empty set?
flashcard.back: When the defining condition is false for all possible elements in the domain.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: No. \(\{\emptyset\}\) contains one element (the empty set), so it is not empty.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: The set containing no elements, denoted \(\emptyset\) or \(\{\}\).
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: A common trick: students think \(\{\emptyset\}\) is empty. Emphasise that \(\{\emptyset\}\) contains the empty set as a member, so its cardinality is 1.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Identify empty sets in different representations
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer D. Positive integers start at 1, so \(x < 1\) has no solutions, giving the empty set.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.question: A set with a definite (limited) number of elements is called a finite set. Which of the following is a finite set?

options.A: The set of all whole numbers
options.D: The set of all real numbers between 0 and 1

questions.explanation: The set of factors of 30 has exactly 8 elements: 1, 2, 3, 5, 6, 10, 15, 30. This is a finite set. Options A, B, and D are infinite: whole numbers go on forever, even integers are infinite, and real numbers between 0 and 1 form an uncountably infinite set.

source_trace.content_anchor: A set which consists of a definite number of elements is called a finite set. A set which is not finite is called an infinite set.

explanations_tiered.definition: A finite set has a countable number of elements; the count is a non-negative integer \(n(A)\).
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Finite sets can be counted completely. Factor sets of integers are always finite because integers have finitely many factors. Whole numbers, even integers, and intervals of real numbers are infinite.
explanations_tiered.beginner: A finite set ends. You can list all elements if you have enough time. The factors of 30 can all be written down; whole numbers cannot.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: The cardinality \(n(A)\) is a finite integer for finite sets. For \(A = \{x: x \mid 30\}\), \(n(A) = 8\), making it finite. Infinite sets like \(\mathbb{W}\) have no largest element.
explanations_tiered.advanced: A set is finite if there exists a bijection from \(\{1,2,\ldots, n\}\) to the set for some \(n \in \mathbb{N}\). The factor set of any integer is finite because the number of divisors is bounded by \(2\sqrt{n}\).
reasoning_steps: Recall definition: finite set has limited number of elements.
Identify the set with a fixed, countable number of members.
Option C: factors of 30 = {1,2,3,5,6,10,15,30} → 8 elements.
Options A, B, D have infinite elements.
misconceptions: Some think all sets described by conditions are finite. Conditions like 'even integer' produce infinite sets.
Confusing 'finite' with 'bounded': the set of real numbers between 0 and 1 is bounded but infinite.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Finite: limited count. Factors of N = finite.

glossary_entry.definition: A set that contains a definite (limited) number of elements.

flashcard.front: What distinguishes a finite set from an infinite set?
flashcard.back: A finite set has a limited number of elements; an infinite set does not.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_deck: Mathematics Unit 1’ — Finite and Infinite Sets
publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Is the set of factors of 30 finite or infinite?
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: A set which consists of a definite number of elements.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Students often think 'bounded interval' means finite. Clarify that [0,1] contains infinitely many real numbers.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Distinguish between finite and infinite sets
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer C. Factors of 30 are \(\{1,2,3,5,6,10,15,30\}\), 8 elements total. Options A, B, D are infinite.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.question: If \(A = \{2, 4, 6, 8, 10, 12\}\), what is \(n(A)\)?
questions.hint: \(n(A)\) means the number of elements in set A.
questions.explanation: The set \(A\) contains the elements 2, 4, 6, 8, 10, and 12. Counting these gives 6 elements. Therefore, \(n(A) = 6\). Option B is correct.

source_trace.content_anchor: n(A) denotes the number of elements of A

explanations_tiered.definition: \(n(A)\) denotes the cardinality (number of elements) of set A.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: For finite sets, \(n(A)\) is simply the count of distinct elements. Set A contains six even numbers from 2 to 12, so \(n(A) = 6\).
explanations_tiered.beginner: Count how many numbers are inside the curly braces. 2,4,6,8,10,12 — that is six numbers.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Cardinality \(n(A)\) measures set size. Listing A explicitly shows 6 elements, so \(n(A)=6\). Option B is correct.
explanations_tiered.advanced: For a finite set, the cardinality function \(n: \mathcal{P}(U) \to \mathbb{N}_0\) assigns a non-negative integer. Here, \(A\) is extensionally defined with 6 distinct elements, so \(n(A)=6\).
reasoning_steps: Count each element once, ignoring duplicates (none here).
The count is 6.
Match 6 to the options.
misconceptions: Some may count the largest element (12) as the cardinality, giving 12 (Option C).
Some may count the difference between largest and smallest (\(12-2=10\), Option D).
explanations_tiered.revision_note: \(n(A)\) = element count. Count, not max value.

glossary_entry.definition: The number of elements in a finite set A, denoted by \(n(A)\).

flashcard.back: The number of elements in set A. For \(A=\{2,4,6,8,10,12\}\), \(n(A)=6\).

publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: 6, because there are six elements in the set.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: The number of elements in a finite set, denoted by \(n(A)\).
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Common errors: students pick the largest element (12) or range (10). Emphasise counting distinct elements.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Determine the cardinality of finite sets
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. Count the elements: 2,4,6,8,10,12 → 6 elements. Option A (5) undercounts, C (12) is the largest element, D (10) is the range.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.question: Two sets are equivalent if there exists a one-to-one correspondence between them. Which pair of sets is equivalent?
questions.hint: Equivalent sets have the same number of elements.
questions.explanation: Equivalent sets have equal cardinality. Set \(\{4,5,6\}\) has 3 elements, and \(\{p,q,r\}\) has 3 elements, so they are equivalent. Option A has 3 and 4 elements, C has 3 and 4, D has 2 and 3, so none of those are equivalent.

source_trace.content_anchor: Two sets A and B are said to be equivalent if there is a one-to-one correspondence between them. two finite sets A and B are equivalent, if and only if they have equal number of elements. n(A) = n(B)

explanations_tiered.definition: Two sets are equivalent if there exists a bijection (one-to-one correspondence) between them.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: For finite sets, equivalence means equal cardinality \(n(A)=n(B)\). The actual elements do not need to be related; only the count matters.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Equivalent sets have the same number of items, like 3 apples and 3 oranges — you can match each apple to one orange.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Finite sets are equivalent iff they have the same number of elements. \(\{4,5,6\}\) and \(\{p,q,r\}\) both have cardinality 3, so a bijection exists (e.g., 4↔p, 5↔q, 6↔r).
explanations_tiered.advanced: Equivalence is an equivalence relation on the class of sets. For finite sets, equivalence classes correspond to natural numbers (cardinalities). The existence of a bijection \(f: A \to B\) does not require any structural similarity beyond cardinality.
reasoning_steps: Find cardinality of each set in each pair.
Pair A: 3 vs 4 → not equal → not equivalent.
Pair B: 3 vs 3 → equal → equivalent.
Pair C: 3 vs 4 → not equivalent.
Pair D: 2 vs 3 → not equivalent.
misconceptions: Some think equivalent sets must have the same elements (confusing with equal sets). Equivalent ≠ equal.
Some think the sets must be of the same type (both numbers, both letters). Equivalent cares only about cardinality.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Equivalent = same count. \(n(A)=n(B)\). Not same elements.

glossary_entry.definition: Two sets that have a one-to-one correspondence between their elements; for finite sets, this means \(n(A) = n(B)\).

flashcard.front: What condition makes two finite sets equivalent?
flashcard.back: They have the same number of elements: \(n(A)=n(B)\).

publishing_metadata.flashcard_deck: Mathematics Unit 1’ — Equivalent vs Equal Sets
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: Yes, because both have 3 elements, so a one-to-one correspondence exists.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Sets that can be placed in one-to-one correspondence; same cardinality.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Key distinction: equal sets have identical elements; equivalent sets have same cardinality. Many students confuse these.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Determine whether two sets are equivalent
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. Both sets have 3 elements → equivalent. Option A: 3 vs 4, C: 3 vs 4, D: 2 vs 3, so not equivalent.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.question: If \(A = \{2, 4, 6, 8\}\) and \(B\) is a set equivalent to \(A\), which of the following could be \(B\)?
questions.hint: \(B\) must have the same number of elements as \(A\).
questions.explanation: Set \(A\) has 4 elements. Equivalent sets have equal cardinality, so \(B\) must also have 4 elements. Option B \(\{a, b, c, d\}\) has 4 elements, making it equivalent to \(A\). Options A, C, and D have 3, 3, and 3 elements respectively.

source_trace.content_anchor: Two finite sets A and B are equivalent, if and only if they have equal number of elements. n(A) = n(B)

explanations_tiered.definition: For finite sets, equivalence means \(n(A) = n(B)\); the sets have the same cardinality.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Set A has cardinality 4. Any set B equivalent to A must also have exactly 4 elements, regardless of what those elements are. Only Option B meets this condition.
explanations_tiered.beginner: A has 4 numbers. Equivalent means same count. Which other set also has 4 things? \(\{a,b,c,d\}\) has 4 letters.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: \(n(A) = 4\). For equivalence, \(n(B)\) must equal 4. Option B \(\{a,b,c,d\}\) has 4 elements, so a bijection exists (e.g., \(2 \mapsto a, 4 \mapsto b, 6 \mapsto c, 8 \mapsto d\)).
explanations_tiered.advanced: Equivalence classes under the relation \(\sim\) are determined by cardinality. \([A]_{\sim} = \{B: |B| = 4\}\). Option B belongs to this class; options A, C, D do not.
reasoning_steps: Count elements in A: \(\{2,4,6,8\} \rightarrow 4\) elements.
For equivalence, B must have exactly 4 elements.
Check each option: A has 3, B has 4, C has 3, D has 3.
Only B has 4 elements, so B is equivalent to A.
misconceptions: Some think equivalent sets must be equal (same elements). Equivalent only requires same count, not same members.
Some think the elements must be of the same type (both numbers, both letters). Equivalent ignores type; only count matters.

glossary_entry.definition: Sets that have the same number of elements; there exists a one-to-one correspondence between them.

flashcard.front: Can a set of numbers be equivalent to a set of letters?
flashcard.back: Yes, if both have the same number of elements. Type of elements does not matter for equivalence.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: If A has 4 elements, how many elements must B have to be equivalent to A?
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Sets that have equal cardinality; a bijection exists between them.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Use this question to reinforce that equivalent does not require identical elements, only identical cardinality.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Identify equivalent sets by comparing cardinalities
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. \(n(A)=4\). Option B has 4 elements, so it is equivalent. Options A, C, D have 3 elements each, so they are not equivalent.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.question: A set A is a subset of set B if every element of A is also an element of B. Which of the following statements is true?
questions.hint: Every element of the first set must be in the second set.
questions.explanation: For \(\{1,2\} \subseteq \{1,2,3\}\), both 1 and 2 are in the second set, so it is true. Option B fails because 3 is not in \(\{1,2\}\). Option C fails because 1 and 2 are not in \(\{3,4\}\). Option D is true (every set is a subset of itself), but A is the intended correct answer as a non-trivial proper subset.

source_trace.content_anchor: A set A is said to be a subset of set B if every element of A is also an element of B. A ⊆ B

explanations_tiered.definition: A ⊆ B means every element of A belongs to B.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: The subset relation requires that all members of the first set are also members of the second set. Option A satisfies this, while options B and C violate it.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Subset means all items in the first group are also in the second group. \(\{1,2\}\)'s items (1 and 2) are both in \(\{1,2,3\}\).
explanations_tiered.intermediate: For \(A \subseteq B\), the condition \(\forall x (x \in A \implies x \in B)\) must hold. In option A, 1∈B and 2∈B, so A⊆B is true.
explanations_tiered.advanced: The subset relation is a partial order: reflexive (A⊆A), antisymmetric, and transitive. Option A is a proper subset (A⊂B), while option D is the reflexive case. The question asks which is true, so both A and D are true, but A is the non-trivial exam answer.
reasoning_steps: Take the first set: \(\{1,2\}\).
Since all elements of first set are in second set, \(\{1,2\} \subseteq \{1,2,3\}\) is true.
misconceptions: Some think subset means the first set must be smaller (proper subset). A set can be a subset of itself.
Some reverse the direction, thinking B⊆A means A⊆B.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: A⊆B: all elements of A are in B.

glossary_entry.definition: Set A is a subset of set B if every element of A is also an element of B, denoted A ⊆ B.

flashcard.back: Every element of A is also an element of B. A is a subset of B.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: True, because both 1 and 2 are in \(\{1,2,3\}\).
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: A set A is a subset of B if every element of A belongs to B.
publishing_metadata.revision_note: Subset: all elements of A ∈ B.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Students often confuse subset direction. Emphasise that A⊆B means B is larger or equal, not the reverse.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Determine whether one set is a subset of another
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer A. \(\{1,2\} \subseteq \{1,2,3\}\) because 1 and 2 are both in the second set. Option B is false (3 missing), C false (1,2 missing), D is true but trivial.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.question: If \(n(A) = 4\), how many subsets does set A have?
questions.hint: The number of subsets of a finite set is \(2^{n(A)}\).
questions.explanation: The number of subsets of a finite set with \(n\) elements is \(2^n\). Here \(n(A) = 4\), so the number of subsets is \(2^4 = 16\). Option C is correct. Options A, B, and D represent \(n\), \(2n\), and \(2^{n+1}\) respectively.

source_trace.content_anchor: If a set A is finite with n elements, then the number of subsets of A is 2^n

explanations_tiered.definition: The total number of subsets of a finite set with cardinality n is \(2^n\), including the empty set and the set itself.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: For each element, there are two choices: include it or not. With 4 elements, the multiplication principle gives \(2 \times 2 \times 2 \times 2 = 2^4 = 16\) subsets.
explanations_tiered.beginner: For each of the 4 elements, you decide yes or no (in or out). That's 2 choices, 4 times: \(2\times2\times2\times2 = 16\).
explanations_tiered.intermediate: The number of subsets is given by the formula \(2^{n(A)}\). Substituting \(n(A)=4\) yields \(2^4 = 16\). This counts all combinations from the empty set to the full set.
explanations_tiered.advanced: The power set \(\mathcal{P}(A)\) has cardinality \(2^{|A|}\) by the binomial theorem: \(\sum_{k=0}^{n} \binom{n}{k} = 2^n\). For \(|A|=4\), \(\sum_{k=0}^4 \binom{4}{k} = 1+4+6+4+1 = 16\).
reasoning_steps: Recall the subset count formula: \(2^{n(A)}\).
Substitute \(n(A) = 4\) into the formula.
Select 16 as the answer.

formula_analysis.name: Number of subsets formula
formula_analysis.derivation: Each element has two choices (include/exclude), so total = \(2^n\).

misconceptions: Some think the number of subsets is \(n(A)\) (Option A) or \(2 \times n(A)\) (Option B).
Some forget to include the empty set, counting only non-empty subsets (15, not an option).

glossary_entry.definition: For a finite set with \(n\) elements, the total number of subsets (including the empty set) is \(2^n\).

flashcard.front: How many subsets does a set with 4 elements have?

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: What is the formula for the number of subsets of a set with \(n\) elements?
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: A finite set with \(n\) elements has exactly \(2^n\) subsets, including the empty set and the set itself.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Use binary representation: each element's inclusion/exclusion corresponds to a bit. 4 bits → 16 patterns.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Calculate the number of subsets of a finite set
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer C. The number of subsets is \(2^{n(A)} = 2^4 = 16\). Option A (4) is \(n(A)\), B (8) is \(2n\), D (32) is \(2^{n+1}\).
